Note to Subscribers - Newsletter Delivery

I have published the daily digest of Best in Energy news and research every business day since 2014 and my own newsletter roughly three times a week since late 2024. The only exceptions are public holidays and about five weeks of annual leave each year. If am going on holiday or otherwise unable to publish for more than one day, I always notify subscribers in advance, usually via a caption at the top of the preceding daily digest. If you suddenly stop receiving emails from me without warning, the most likely reason is your subscription has expired or your email system has begun to refuse delivery.

Distributing newsletters via email involves playing a constantly evolving cat-and-mouse game with corporate email security systems designed to counter spam, phishing and other malicious activity. The systems are set up to block and refuse delivery to anything that looks like spam and can be triggered by individual words in the text or simply the fact multiple people in the same organisation receive the same email at the same time. Every time spam filters are upgraded, which happens several times each year, there is a risk emails that were previously delivered safely will start to be refused.

Like other newsletter publishers, I use bulk mailing software (in my case a system called MailerLite). If your email system refuses delivery more than about half a dozen times consecutively, the mailing software will automatically remove your address from the mailing list with no input from me and no warning. This is an anti-spam measure and a legal requirement in most countries designed to prevent abuse.

To ensure uninterrupted delivery of the daily digest and my newsletters, please consider adding my email address (the one used to send this email john@jkempenergy.com) to your contacts or safe senders list. If you suddenly stop receiving emails from me, check your spam filter, speak with your corporate email department, or reach out to me and I will investigate. The problem of non-delivery can almost always be fixed but only once we are aware of it.

Very best wishes
John

Copper inventories monitored by the three major futures exchanges fell by 54.7 kt last week to 1.088 million tons, extending the recent drawdown trend and reinforcing signs of tightening availability outside the United States. The decline was led by the SHFE, where stocks dropped 44.3 kt to 144 kt, the lowest level in six months, while LME-monitored inventories fell by a further 12 kt. In contrast, COMEX stocks rose by 1.6 kt, marking a tenth consecutive weekly increase as metal continues to be drawn into the US ahead of a potential copper tariff announcement expected by the end of June.

The divergence highlights the ongoing regional dislocation in the copper market. Traders and merchants have spent months redirecting material towards the US in anticipation of import restrictions, helping lift COMEX inventories to record levels while simultaneously draining stocks elsewhere. As a result, COMEX now accounts for 54% of all exchange-monitored copper inventories, a remarkable shift from historical norms, especially considering the nations relative small consumption level compared with the rest of the world.

Despite the recent increase in visible stocks, the broader market remains focused on a tightening medium-term outlook. The continued drawdown across Shanghai and London suggests underlying physical demand remains resilient, while concerns about mine supply growth, electrification demand, and the potential impact of US trade measures continue to support a constructive longer-term outlook for copper.

Over the past seven weeks, managed money's net long position in Brent crude has collapsed from a 7½-year high of 496k contracts to a 2026 low at 114k contracts in the reporting week ending 16 June, when prospects of a US-Iran peace deal gathered momentum and Brent subsequently tumbled 14%. The scale of the liquidation highlights how quickly speculative sentiment has swung from fears of a prolonged supply disruption to expectations of a sizeable supply surge once flows through the Strait of Hormuz normalise.

However, as highlighted previously, last week's 45% reduction in the net long was driven primarily by aggressive short selling rather than long liquidation. Gross short positions surged to 231k contracts, the highest level since the pandemic, while long exposure remained relatively elevated. This leaves the market increasingly vulnerable to a short-covering rally should the expected supply recovery prove slower than currently priced, or if traders are forced to reassess what given the present situation constitutes a fundamentally justified crude price.

NOTE: News Headlines Can Change This Outlook Before Market Open. - Oil Remains News Sensitive....

Top News Story: Volatility Rocks Crude as JD Vance's Comments Spark Fresh Doubts Over U.S.-Iran Peace Durability

Crude oil prices are seeing a highly volatile reset as the market slides down into a new trading channel, with WTI settling near $75.50/bbl following consecutive weeks of heavy liquidation. 

While an interim U.S.-Iran diplomatic truce has allowed some long-stranded physical vessels to begin moving through the Strait of Hormuz, optimism hit a major roadblock over the weekend. 

Comments from Vice President JD Vance raised serious questions regarding the stability and long term durability of the peace agreement, injecting fresh geopolitical anxiety back into the complex just as traders expected a smooth return of waterborne logistics. 

Compounding this friction, reports over the weekend indicate that Tehran is threatening to slow the implementation of the waterway reopening unless specific regional ceasefires are fully respected and critical oil export waivers are formally signed. Automated trading programs have rapidly shifted behavior, abandoning the straight-line capitulation dump to brace for a highly fragmented, headline-sensitive trading band.

Asymmetric Risk: Path of least resistance has turned neutral-to-bearish for near-term contracts. Algorithmic systems are aggressively fading minor intraday bounces, keeping a heavy lid on prompt delivery pricing until formal signatures are finalized.

The "Zero Liquidity" Trap: Depth of book remains highly fragile over the weekend open. Wide bid-ask spreads mean any sudden pushback or conflicting statements from diplomatic envoys regarding the deal's exact terms will trigger immediate $3–$4 vacuum spikes.

Verification Dynamics: Traders are trapped in a highly reactive news cycle. Automated systems are executing purely on raw political feeds, leaving physical inventory fundamentals secondary to the micro-level diplomatic tape.

Social Media Buzz: The View from X
Energy networks are experiencing total volatility as commentators dissect the conflicting political reports surrounding the permanence of the maritime accord.

(Oil On The Tracks): Satellite tracking accounts are working overtime, noting that while some idled tanker capacity has begun to clear, a massive concentration of vessels remains anchored outside the chokepoint waiting for official clear signals.

The Technical Breakdown: Chart-focused accounts are sounding the alarm, noting that WTI has established thick overhead resistance near the $79.20 zone, validating a broader consolidation channel.

Agricultural Relief: Farm and agribusiness handles are highlighting the ongoing slide in fuel futures, noting the immediate cost relief this localized flush continues to deliver for mid-summer regional operating budgets.

Macro Volatility: Speculation is building that if energy inputs remain suppressed within this lower band, central banks will have a clear runway to revive deferred interest rate cuts before the end of the summer.

Shipment & Logistics Tracker: Monitoring Critical Choke Points

Suez Canal: CONDITIONAL RE-ENTRY (Impact: High) Fleet operators are closely analyzing the weekend political friction, keeping container routing adjustments tentative until long-term security parameters are fully validated.

Cape of Good Hope: TON-MILE COOLING (Impact: Moderate) Extreme maritime premiums are cooling off as standard shipping networks prepare for a gradual migration back toward northern arteries, softening global transit penalties.

Panama Canal: PRESSURE BALANCING (Impact: Low) Wait times are stabilizing as global freight routing expectations normalize away from emergency transshipment detours.

Supply & Production Intelligence
Stockpile Dynamics: Goldman Sachs highlights that even as oil flows slowly begin to normalize, global country reserves are heavily depleted from the spring crisis, which will require steady restocking through late 2026.

OPEC+ Tracker: The market is realizing that scheduled voluntary production increases will now land on an opening, expanding spot supply chain, further dampening prompt pricing.

Secondary Shocks: Severe deflation in input costs is flowing through to fertilizer and energy-heavy manufacturing components, lowering inflation indicators.

Price Summary and Recent Movements
WTI has officially broken its multi-month structural uptrend, erasing nearly all premium metrics accumulated since late February. The market structure has sharply adjusted, transitioning from extreme backwardation to a flatter, highly stable pricing curve as refiners pause immediate prompt-month bidding wars.

NYMEX Data (WTI/bbl): The prompt contract closed at a definitive $75.50/bbl on Friday. Crucially, the steep backwardation that defined the spring market is collapsing rapidly, with prompt delivery premiums flattening as the immediate scramble for waterborne inventory cools.

The technical and fundamental bias is geared toward channel consolidation in the near term, entirely tethered to the formal signing and execution of the chokepoint reopening. 

Traders must manage severe headline gap risk over the weekend session open.

Global equities edged lower in holiday-thinned trading as investors assessed whether a 60-day diplomatic window between the US and Iran could pave the way for a broader nuclear agreement and a more durable peace settlement.

MSCI's global equity index fell 0.2%, trimming weekly gains, while an Asian stock gauge slipped 0.9% after a five-day rally that had carried the benchmark to record highs. S&P 500 futures declined 0.5% following Thursday's 1.1% advance, and the Dollar strengthened against most major peers as investors adopted a more cautious stance heading into the weekend. Market liquidity was reduced by holiday closures across the US, China, Hong Kong and Taiwan.

Brent crude traded below $80/bbl and was on course for a weekly decline of roughly 9%. The sharp drop reflects easing concerns over global energy supplies after the US-Iran interim peace agreement helped restore shipping activity through the Strait of Hormuz, unwinding much of the conflict-related risk premium in oil markets.

Bond yields moved higher across parts of the Asia-Pacific region, with 10-year Japanese and Australian yields advancing. In the US, two-year Treasury yields steadied around 4.18% after surging 13 bps in the previous session to their highest level in more than a year as traders increased bets on future Fed tightening following the central bank's hawkish hold.

Gold was on track for a third consecutive weekly loss as expectations for higher interest rates outweighed optimism surrounding the peace agreement. Adding to the pressure, Goldman Sachs cut its year-end gold price forecast by $500/oz, reflecting a less supportive outlook for the precious metal amid rising real yields and a stronger Dollar.

TRUMP TO IRAN: “CLOSE HORMUZ AND YOU WON’T HAVE A COUNTRY”

President Trump revealed that he warned Iranian leaders against closing the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world’s most strategically important shipping routes. His message underscores the enormous economic and geopolitical consequences that could result from disrupting a corridor through which a significant portion of global oil exports flows every day.

The Strait of Hormuz is more than a regional chokepoint—it is a critical artery of the global economy. Any threat to its operation immediately raises concerns about energy supply, inflation, shipping costs, and global market stability. This is why tensions surrounding Hormuz are closely monitored by governments, investors, and central banks alike.

For markets, the issue extends far beyond politics. The stability of key trade routes remains essential to global growth, and any disruption can rapidly impact oil prices, investor sentiment, and economic expectations worldwide. What impact would it have on the civilian population of Iran?

What is the likelihood President Trump will actually order this action if peace talks fail to deter Iran's development and pursuit of a nuclear weapon?

GROK says:

Significant but incomplete degradation of Iran’s military effectiveness, with major humanitarian consequences for civilians. Trump has repeatedly and explicitly threatened precisely these actions (destroying power plants and bridges) as leverage in the ongoing 2026 crisis, most prominently in April statements framing “Power Plant Day, and Bridge Day” with plans to decimate “every bridge in Iran” and leave “every power plant in Iran... burning, exploding and never to be used again” over a short period (e.g., four hours) if demands on the Strait of Hormuz and deals were not met.
https://t.co/TfJoR878TZ

Fox News reporting has covered these ultimatums in detail as strategic pressure on the regime’s power structures and anchors (tied to IRGC/generals maintaining control), while noting dual-use aspects like communications, water systems, and industrial production.
https://t.co/TfJoR878TZ

Impacts on Iran’s Military Capabilities
Iran’s military (regular forces + IRGC) emphasizes asymmetric warfare, proxies, ballistic/cruise missiles, drones, and a decentralized “mosaic defense” doctrine. This was deliberately built to survive U.S.-style decapitation strikes, comms disruption, and infrastructure attacks—dispersed units, pre-positioned stockpiles, provincial autonomy, and redundancy.
https://t.co/rTcDT5WJE2

Ability to carry out military attacks: 
Moderately to significantly reduced, but not eliminated. Power loss would impair centralized command-and-control (C2), radar/air defense networks, some munitions production facilities, and coordination of complex operations. However, mobile launchers, hardened or dispersed sites, and pre-planned strikes allow continued missile/drone attacks on U.S./allied forces, shipping, or Israel in the short-to-medium term. Proxies (e.g., Houthis, Iraqi militias) operate with significant autonomy and local supplies, so their attacks would be less affected. Sustained, high-volume campaigns would suffer from degraded      support infrastructure.

Resupply      of ground forces: 
Substantially hampered. Bridges are critical chokepoints for road and rail networks moving troops, fuel, ammunition, and equipment across Iran’s terrain (rivers, mountains, deserts). Widespread destruction      would create bottlenecks, force long detours, increase vulnerability to follow-on strikes, and slow logistics dramatically. Iran’s doctrine prioritizes proxies and asymmetric ops over large-scale conventional      ground maneuvers, so this hurts more for any defensive repositioning or internal security than for offensive projection. Air/sea alternatives or      engineering repairs (pontoons, etc.) exist but are slower and limited under sustained pressure.

Communication with allied military forces (proxies, regional partners): 
Disrupted for centralized systems but resilient overall. Power outages would hit landlines, cellular networks, and internet-dependent C2. Forces would shift to radio, satellite comms (harder to fully disrupt), couriers, and pre-established decentralized networks. Iran’s mosaic structure empowers local/provincial IRGC commanders and proxies to act independently if      central links are severed. Coordination frictions would increase (slower decision-making, reduced synchronization), but operational continuity would persist better than in a centralized military.

Net military effect: 
These strikes would impose real costs and friction, degrading sustainment and large-scale ops more than quick asymmetric strikes. They align with standard targeting of dual-use infrastructure that enables an adversary’s war effort (as Fox framing suggests, hitting regime power anchors). However, Iran’s resilience design means it would not be a decisive knockout—attacks and proxy activity could continue at reduced intensity.
https://t.co/TfJoR878TZ

Impacts on the Civilian Population

Severe and widespread humanitarian consequences. Widespread or nationwide blackouts would immediately affect:

Hospitals and medical care (loss of power for equipment, refrigeration of medicines/blood, surgery).

Water supply (pumping, treatment, and desalination plants—Trump has also threatened these).

Food storage/distribution and refrigeration → spoilage and shortages.

Daily life in a hot climate (no AC, lighting, cooking, communications).

Broader economy (factories, businesses, jobs grind to a halt) → shortages, inflation, and hardship.

Longer-term risks include health crises, displacement, and potential instability or unrest. The regime has used human shields around power plants in response to threats and could exploit suffering for propaganda to rally domestic support or portray the U.S. as attacking civilians. Fox reporting notes cascading civilian effects alongside the strategic pressure on the regime.
https://t.co/TfJoR878TZ

Right-leaning perspectives (as reflected in Fox coverage of Trump’s statements) generally view these as regrettable but necessary consequences of targeting infrastructure that supports the regime’s military aggression and nuclear ambitions. The regime bears primary responsibility for endangering its population through policies that invite such pressure, in contrast to Iran’s support for proxies causing civilian harm elsewhere. Precision U.S. munitions aim to limit collateral damage relative to indiscriminate alternatives.

Likelihood Trump Orders This If Peace Talks Fail to Deter Nuclear Pursuit

Low to moderate for a comprehensive, simultaneous “every power plant and every bridge” campaign; higher for more targeted or graduated strikes on key dual-use nodes.

Trump has issued these exact threats multiple times (extensively reported by Fox News) as maximum-pressure leverage but has a consistent pattern of extending deadlines, pausing planned strikes for talks/back-channel diplomacy, and pursuing deals/MOUs even after strong rhetoric.
https://t.co/TfJoR878TZ

If talks collapse specifically because Iran continues advancing its nuclear program (a clear red line in Trump’s past statements and current negotiations), the probability of escalated military action rises. This      could include strikes on power generation or bridges supporting nuclear-related facilities, missile production, or logistics.

However, a blanket, rapid destruction of all such infrastructure is less likely. It is highly escalatory (risking major Iranian retaliation on      shipping/proxies/cyber, global oil shock, wider war). It contradicts America First priorities of avoiding quagmires and new long-term commitments. Trump has shown preference for calibrated pressure +      diplomacy that forces concessions rather than total destruction.

Right-leaning sources like Fox frame the threats as focused on regime power structures to achieve strategic goals (e.g., Strait access, nuclear limits) rather than gratuitous harm. Expect renewed ultimatums, targeted strikes on high-value military/nuclear-enabling targets, continued naval leverage on the Strait, and diplomatic off-ramps over a full “Bridge and Power Day” operation.

In short, infrastructure strikes remain a credible tool in Trump’s toolkit if diplomacy fails on nukes, but history and incentives point toward selective/pressure-oriented application rather than the most apocalyptic version threatened. Events remain fluid—monitor official statements for shifts.

In East Asia, the agreement could signal to Taiwan doubts about firm, long-term U.S. support in a crisis with China.

In Europe, Canada, and Australia, Trump’s policies—including comments on annexing Greenland and Canada—combined with this agreement, could increase desires to reduce strategic dependence on America. The structural consequence is underweighted in current oil forward curves. "No tolls" was never formally codified in the MOU (the memorandum of understanding between the US and Iran), which means there's no binding enforcement mechanism if Iran later decides to impose access fees on non-American tankers. Any OPEC+ member — Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iraq — exporting through the strait now does so subject to an implicit Iranian veto on access. That's an informal tax on roughly 20 million barrels per day of global flow, and the futures market hasn't priced a permanent risk premium for it.

A walkout is a negotiating move, not a breakdown — the real signal is Hormuz transit counts over the next 48 hours. If tankers keep moving, the walkout is theater and oil at roughly $80 (Brent crude, the international benchmark) is right to price it that way. If IRGC enforcement visibly tightens and vessel transits slow, that's the move the market isn't pricing: a push toward $90+ with no room for OPEC+ to offset it, since the cartel is already near its voluntary production limit.
